title: Pentland, Cross Lane, Northallerton
bathrooms: Not on record — verify with a viewing or the listing
summary: Pentland is a substantial four-bedroom detached house occupying a premium position in the rural village of Ingleby Arncliffe. The property recently transacted in April 2026 for £672,400, establishing a high benchmark for Cross Lane driven by its expansive 212 square metre footprint. Situated at the foot of the North York Moors, the location offers exceptional countryside access alongside rapid commuter links via the nearby A19 and A172. While the property benefits from a very low crime rate and freehold tenure, the EPC rating is capped at a potential Band D, suggesting inherent energy efficiency limitations. This report serves as an asset baseline for the current owners or intelligence for buyers monitoring the street for future listings.

areaAnalysis.demographics: A mature, affluent rural demographic comprising established families, professionals, and retirees drawn to the National Park lifestyle.
amenities: The Joiners Shop Coffee & Kitchen (Ingleby Cross)
Extensive walking routes into the North York Moors
Broader retail and medical facilities in Stokesley (7 miles)
areaAnalysis.futureOutlook: The area is highly protected from overdevelopment due to its proximity to the National Park. Capital values are expected to remain robust, driven by scarcity of supply and sustained demand for premium rural homes.

buyingSuitability: As this property recently transacted in April 2026 and is not currently on the market, this report serves as an asset baseline for the current owners, a remortgage valuation guide, or intelligence for prospective buyers monitoring the street for future listings. The £672,400 achieved price establishes a high ceiling for Cross Lane, underpinned by the substantial 212 square metre footprint and prime rural location. Future purchasers should note the limited EPC potential (Band D) and budget for potential energy efficiency upgrades if it returns to market.
viewingChecks: Inspect the primary heating system (oil tank, LPG, or heat pump) and check the boiler's service history.
Verify the number of bathrooms and their condition, as this is absent from public records.
Check the quality and age of the windows, given the EPC Band D rating.
Assess the roof condition and any recent maintenance, common requirements for older rural properties.
Test mobile phone reception across different networks while inside the property.
Review the boundary lines, fences, and any shared access points on the driveway.
Look for any signs of damp or condensation, particularly in ground-floor reception rooms.
Check the loft space for insulation depth and potential for further boarding or conversion.
Inspect the drainage system (mains sewer vs septic tank/cesspit) given the rural setting.
Assess the garden's orientation and natural light exposure throughout the day.
agentQuestions: If valuing for a return to market, how does the £672,400 benchmark compare to current local demand?
What is the primary heating fuel, and when was the system last upgraded?
Are there any restrictive covenants or National Park fringe regulations affecting the title?
Is the property connected to mains drainage, or does it rely on a septic tank?
Can you confirm the exact number of bathrooms and en-suites?
Why is the EPC potential capped at Band D, and what specific barriers prevent a higher rating?
Have any structural alterations or extensions been completed since the 2026 purchase?
What are the current broadband speeds achieved at the property?
Are there any known surface water flooding issues in the immediate garden or driveway?
Who maintains the boundaries and any shared access routes on Cross Lane?

recentlySoldPanel.headline: Recently sold — what this means
bullets: This property completed around Apr 2026 at £672,400. It is not currently available to purchase.
Treat £672,400 as the strongest recent signal for this exact address when judging street and area values.
If it returns to market, re-check EPC, planning, survey condition and fresh same-street solds before setting any offer band.
Ask how the previous sale was marketed, whether works completed after completion, and whether any survey issues surfaced.
Budget for stamp duty/LBTT (and ADS if relevant), solicitor, survey and moving costs on top of any future purchase price.
For remortgage or sale planning, instruct a local agent or valuer with this report and the Land Registry sale history.

livingHere.vignette: A Saturday morning in Cross Lane often begins with a short walk to pick up everyday essentials from the nearby Londis. From there, residents can head outdoors for a walk through Swinegar Plantation or join the Cleveland Way national trail just a mile from home. Afterwards, it is a brief stroll to The Joiners Shop for a morning coffee, or slightly further to The Rusty Bike. For a more substantial weekend lunch, locals might gather at The Blue Bell Inn, located just a fifth of a mile away.
